description: The common problems of the aged and their families are studied, knowledge of existing services is provided, and students are exposed to the initial theory and practice of delivering services to the aged in both the outpatient and inpatient setting.

title: Race and Minority Relations
description: Examines the history and status of minority groups in society; the relationship between minority groups and the dominant group; focusing on the causes of intergroup conflict and solutions to intergroup problems. Prerequisite: SOC 100 or SOC 250 or permission

title: Sociology of Family
description: A sociological analysis of the family as a social unit. Topics focus on historical changes, cultural patterns, alterations in gender roles as they affect family roles, social class influences, group processes in families, institutional relationships with the polity, economy, religious, and economic institutions, and the future of the family. Prerequisite: SOC 100 or SOC 250 or permission

title: Sociology of Religion
description: Examines religion as a social phenomenon and attempts to relate it to other aspects of human social life. Topics include the dilemmas of belief and institutionalization, religious movements, religion and change, secularization, and the future of religion. Prerequisite: SOC 100 or SOC 250 or permission

title: Deviance and Social Control
description: The philosophical assumptions embedded in the theories of deviance are examined and applied in the critical reading of deviance texts. A basic question explored is who and what are defined as deviant by each of the sociological perspectives. Through this examination we will investigate who controls social behavior and the basis of control contained within the theories. Topics include crime, mental illness, victimless crime, and elite/corporate deviance. Prerequisite: SOC 100 or SOC 250 or permission

title: Sociology of Health and Illness
description: This course provides an introduction to the field of medical sociology with attention to physical as well as mental illnesses. The emphasis will be upon the influence of social factors in becoming ill and social factors which influence treatment. Topics to be covered will include: epidemiology, health and illness behavior, the health professions, health care institutions, and alternative systems of health care. Prerequisite: SOC 100 or SOC 250 or permission

title: Seminar in Social Psychology
description: This course is designed to investigate the effect of various social factors and situations on the perceptions and behavior of humans. Analysis of the conceptual tools for investigating human activity and applications of the theoretical and conceptual perspectives will be considered as they are related to social settings and organizations where humans interact.

title: Directed Research     1-3 credit hours
description: Independent original research of a selected topic in sociology under the direction of a sociology graduate faculty member. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00 Prerequisite: SOC 237 or permission

title: Thesis     3-6 credit hours
description: Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Directed Readings     1-3 credit hours
description: Independent readings on advanced sociological topics. Readings are to be selected and directed by a sociology graduate faculty member. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00 Prerequisite: Two 300 or 400 level Sociology courses or permission

title: Golden Age Drama
description: A study of the dramatists of the Golden Age in Spain.

title: Structure of the Spanish Language
description: A review of advanced Spanish grammar with special emphasis on applied and/or socio-linguistics and issues related to Spanish instruction. Prerequisite: Admission into the MAE in Spanish program

title: Comparative Grammar
description: A comparative and contrastive study of the structures of Spanish and English.

title: Oral, Written, & Cultural Proficiency
description: Communicative practice to improve oral and written proficiency and expand knowledge of cultures in the Spanish-speaking world.

title: Seminar in Contemporary Drama
description: Study of the most important present-day dramatists writing in Spanish.

title: Modern Spanish Poetry
description: Survey of Spanish and Latin American poetry of the present day.

title: Latin American Literature
description: Survey of representative works of Latin American literature from its beginning to the present period.

title: Cervantes
description: The principal works of Cervantes with special emphasis on Don Quixote.

title: Topics in Peninsular Literature and Culture
description: In this course, students will have the opportunity to explore various genres of texts by Spanish artists and authors. Topic to be selected and announced in schedule of courses. May be taken more than once. Total Credits Allowed: 9.00 Prerequisite: Admission into the MAE in Spanish program

title: Topics in Latin American Literature and Culture
description: The study of selected works in Latin American literature and culture. Topic to be selected and announced in schedule of courses. May be taken more than once. Total Credits Allowed: 9.00 Prerequisite: Admission into the MAE in Spanish program

title: Topics in U.S. Latino/Chicano Literature and Culture
description: The study of selected works in U.S. Latino/Chicano literature and culture. Topic to be selected and announced in schedule of courses. May be taken more than once. Total Credits Allowed: 9.00

title: Topics in Spanish Linguistics/Applied Linguistics
description: The study of selected topics in Spanish Linguistics, Sociolinguistics and/or Applied Linguistics. Topic to be selected and announced in schedule of courses. May be taken more than once.  No more than 9 credits of SPAN 855 may apply to the degree program. Total Credits Allowed: 9.00

title: Spanish Heritage Language Pedagogy
description: Theory and practice of teaching Spanish as a heritage language.

title: Seminar in Spanish/ Latin American Studies
description: The study of literary works and cultural contexts. Topic to be selected and announced in schedule of courses. May be taken more than once. Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Content and Language Integrated Instructional Methods for Dual Language/Bilingual Education
description: This course prepares pre-service and in-service bilingual educators to effectively integrate language and content instruction in PK-12 bilingual/dual-immersion programs. Participants will explore the history and policies of bilingual education in the U.S., research on the benefits of bilingualism, and language development theories for emergent bilinguals. Emphasis is placed on aligning instruction and assessment with PK-12 content standards while addressing the unique language needs of bilingual learners. This course is taught in Spanish.

title: Graduate Study Abroad
description: A study of the language and culture of Hispanic countries. This study will be conducted in a Spanish-speaking country; it also requires a research paper to be presented to UNK graduate faculty. Prerequisite: SPAN 814 or permission of graduate chair

title: Graduate Study Abroad
description: A study of the language and culture of Hispanic countries. This study will be conducted in a Spanish-speaking country; it also requires a research paper to be presented to UNK graduate faculty. Prerequisite: SPAN 814 or permission of graduate chair

title: Professional Development
description: Spanish professional development sequence. Prerequisite: Any 800-level course in Spanish.

title: Thesis     1-6 credit hours
description: Thesis supervised by faculty advisor and thesis committee. Department Consent Required Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Directed Readings in Spanish     1-3 credit hours
description: Individual studies and research under the guidance of a graduate faculty member. May be taken twice for two separate studies or research approved by the graduate faculty in the specific language. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00 Prerequisite: Any 800 level course in Spanish or Spanish-American literature

title: Special Topics     1-3 credit hours
description: Special topics to be determined by the Department of Modern Languages. May be taken more than once. Total Credits Allowed: 18.00 Prerequisite: Admission into the MAE in Spanish program

title: Independent Studies in Spanish     1-3 credit hours
description: Individual studies and research under the guidance of a faculty member. May be taken twice for two separate studies or research in Spanish. Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Special Topics in Speech Communications     1-3 credit hours
description: Course work on a specific topic of communication designed by the instructor. Topics vary per offering. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00

title: Introduction to Graduate Study in Communication
description: The purpose of this course is to introduce students to the study of communication at the graduate level and to prepare students for success in the graduate program.  We will focus on the nature of communication as a field of study; on skills which will assist you in future courses; and on the expectations and requirements for completion of the graduate program.  This course is designed, in part, to familiarize students with the faculty in the graduate program and to assist students in identifying potential advisors for their thesis or practicum.

title: Strategic Communication
description: This course will analyze and explore the multifaceted world of public relations and strategic communication. It will focus on the general characteristics and functions of the industry along with the specific responsibilities of PR and Communication professionals.

title: Health Communication
description: This course provides an overview and introduction to health communication.  We will cover the breadth of health communication, including interpersonal health communication, patient-provider communication, and persuasive health messaging. This course will give students a foundation in health communication research, exposing them to the breadth of the field. Through course readings and assignments students will explore health communication, including interpersonal health communication, patient-provider communication, and persuasive health messaging. Assignments in this class will include papers, tests, and application (designing theory-based promotions). By the end of the semester, students will be able to engage with health communication as a field, have a basic understanding of multiple theories utilized in health communication; understanding the basic constructs and be able to apply the theories correctly in both research and program design (health education/promotion programs), and will have a grasp at the complexity of health communication.

title: Administration and Direction of Forensic Programs
description: Organization and administration of forensic programs in high schools and colleges, management of inter-school contests and tournaments, coaching of debate, discussion and individual events, and principles of judging.

title: Interpersonal Communication
description: This course will provide an introduction to and survey of communication research in interpersonal communication.  We will discuss the basic components of interpersonal communication (e.g., goals, message production, verbal and nonverbal communication), theories of interpersonal communication, and interpersonal communication in context.

title: Social Movements and International Media
description: An exploration of theories and principles essential to understanding the communicative function and process of social movements. Special attention will be paid to understanding and learning how to construct persuasive messages intended to produce social change.

title: Teaching of Communication Studies
description: The purpose of the course is to prepare the graduate student to teach communication courses, specifically Public Speaking, in an academic environment and/or training program of applied communication.  To this end, time management, the learning environment, assessment, and current research in Instructional Communication will be presented and explored.

title: Science Communication
description: Course examines science communication from multiple perspectives, including the exchange of ideas by science professionals; cross-disciplinary approaches for communicating science to broad audiences; perceptions of science as conveyed by mass media; and roles of science communication in society.

title: Diffusion of Innovations
description: Diffusion of Innovations examines the adoption of innovations by individuals in a social system from the perspective of a communication based model. Examples of innovations include fashion trends, campaign slogans, communication technologies, hygiene practices, birth control, farming practices, hybrid powered automobiles, types of government rule (democracy) and genetically engineered foods. The diffusion phenomenon is examined emphasizing social and individual points of view. Class sessions are mostly based on lecture, with some discussion. Assessment tools include exams, case studies, and a final semester project. The final project will be presented to the class.

title: Introduction to Communication Research
description: The purpose of this course is to broadly introduce students to qualitative and quantitative research approaches in the field of communication. To achieve this, students will read and interpret communication scholarship. Additionally, we will investigate ethical strategies for conducting research, including the issues of sampling, data collection, and data analysis techniques. Finally, we will discuss options for writing up and presenting research through exploration of various dissemination outlets.

title: Language and Social Change
description: The course introduces students to the role of information, communication and the media in development and social change. To put development into context, the course looks at theories in development and how these have influenced the different development communication approaches used at various times. Students will explore: the concept of participatory communication; global debates about development; the digital divide; development policy frameworks at the global, regional and national levels. Communication of issues pertinent to sustainable development will be addressed including environment, population, gender, poverty and conflict management.

title: Leadership Communication
description: In today's dynamic, global work environment, leaders need to be able to communicate effectively with people located in the same geographical location or located anywhere across the globe. This course offers students an opportunity to evaluate and utilize numerous communication options to develop an authentic, powerful, leadership voice using interpersonal, technology-mediated, and intercultural communication competencies and techniques.

title: Advanced Organizational Communication
description: Students in this course will investigate a variety of significant organizational communication issues that contribute to the development and maintenance of the organizational workplace. Students will apply theories through case studies and projects.

title: Interviewing
description: Theories and techniques of interviewing, including information gathering, employment, appraisal, and persuasive interviewing.

title: Intercultural Communication
description: Study of communication across cultures.

title: Communication Training and Consulting
description: A study of how to plan, conduct, and evaluate communication training and development programs within the organization.

title: Rhetorical Theory
description: This course provides an overview of the history, theory, and practice of rhetoric from classical times to the contemporary. The course highlights key areas of controversy within rhetorical theory structured as a survey of theoretical agreements and disagreements for students to better appreciate what is at stake in rhetorical theory.

title: Directed Studies     1-4 credit hours
description: Independent research or special assignment in the student's major field. Total Credits Allowed: 4.00

title: Internship     1-6 credit hours
description: Practical experience that fuses communication theory and practice in a professional setting. Department Consent Required Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Public Communication Practicum     1-3 credit hours
description: Students will connect their graduate curriculum to professional experience in an effort to demonstrate an ability to apply coursework within an organization. Students will develop a completion paper that explains accomplishments in the practicum experience and connects these accomplishments to the graduate curriculum. Students will present their practicum findings to faculty. Department Consent Required Total Credits Allowed: 3.00

title: Public Communication Thesis     1-6 credit hours
description: In conjunction with their advisor, students will prepare a research project based on graduate coursework. The student's advisor and two additional graduate faculty will serve as the student's thesis committee. The committee will assemble for an oral defense of the thesis. Department Consent Required Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Quantitative Research
description: This class focuses on the introductory statistical techniques used in social science research. Students will be introduced to concepts such as reliability, validity, measures of central tendency, variability, probability, and statistical techniques including: t-tests (independent & dependent samples), Analysis of variance, Chi-square, correlation, and regression. Students are expected to take the material/concepts presented in class and apply them through a series of homework assignments and quizzes. The overall goal of the course is to help students understand the mathematical/statistical concepts presented and to assist in the application of these procedures.

title: Rhetorical Criticism
description: This graduate seminar examines the use of rhetoric as a critical lens and a methodology to analyze artifacts. The class follows Kenneth Burkes approach of looking at life as drama (dramatism) that can be analyzed to better understand symbols and discourse around us. In addition to delving into theories of rhetorical criticism as a method (who gets to say what about what and to whom with what purpose?), we will also learn tools to examine the symbolicity of speeches, films, spaces, stories, culture, politics, etc. The class will take two approaches: to understand and critique exemplars of criticism and to give you the tools to perform criticism yourselves. In the dynamic space between subjective and objective, between author and audience, and between beauty and form lies the rhetorical critic.

title: Qualitative Research Methodology
description: This course is focused on teaching students how to understand and conduct qualitative communication research. Special attention will be given to the philosophical assumptions behind qualitative research as well as the tools necessary to conduct. The class will also focus not only on the tools necessary to conduct research, but also on the epistemological questions of how knowledge is generated through research.

title: Theories of Interpersonal Communication
description: A survey of major theories and research in interpersonal communication.

title: Seminar in Speech
description: Concentrated study of selected topics in speech communication. (May be repeated for a total of 6 hours.)

title: Thesis     1-6 credit hours
description: This course provides guidance for a student to work on his/her master's thesis, which is the culminating experience in the UNK Public Communication program thesis option. Students will work with a Thesis Adviser to develop, research and prepare an original research project building on the curriculum. The project will conclude with a defense of the project to the student's thesis committee. Department Consent Required Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Applied Communication Theory
description: This course will cover the breadth of communication theory.  We will discuss different philosophical approaches to theory; communication theories in a variety of contexts; and application of theories in every-day life.  We will discuss communication theories in the contexts of intrapersonal, interpersonal, relational, group, organizational, mediated, and mass communication contexts, among others.  The course will emphasize the application of theories to improve understanding of our own and others' communication and to assist in strategic communication planning.

title: Communication Theory
description: A study of social and scientific theories of interpersonal, group, organizational, and mass communication.

title: Independent Study     1-3 credit hours
description: Individual studies and research under the guidance of a faculty member. May be taken twice for two separate studies. Department Consent Required Total Credits Allowed: 3.00

title: Foundations of Research: Methodologies and Practices for Educational Inquiry
description: This web-based graduate course introduces students to the principles and practices of action research as a powerful tool for improving teaching and learning. Participants will learn to critically evaluate educational research and apply its methods and findings to their own classrooms and professional settings. The course emphasizes the cyclical process of identifying a problem of practice, gathering and analyzing data, implementing informed changes, and reflecting upon outcomes to guide continual improvement. Through this course, educators will strengthen their ability to use research as a means for reflection, innovation, and sustained professional growth.

title: The Dynamic Relationship Between Philosophy and Education
description: This graduate course explores the major philosophical traditions and questions that have shaped educational thought and practice. Students will explore major schools of thought in the philosophical tradition. Through reading and discussion of classic and contemporary texts, students will engage critically with the ideas of key philosophers, examining how differing conceptions of knowledge, truth, ethics, and human flourishing inform educational aims, curriculum, pedagogy, and policy. The course emphasizes the role of philosophy in analyzing educational purposes, equity, and the moral dimensions of teaching and learning. Through readings, discussion, and applied analysis, students will develop the ability to articulate and defend their own educational philosophy while situating it within broader philosophical and sociocultural contexts. The course invites participants to reflect deeply on what it means to educate, to learn, and to teach in a complex and pluralistic world.

title: Curriculum Development in Multicultural Education
description: Students learn to evaluate, create, and implement multicultural lesson plans, unit plans, textbooks, curriculum guides, classroom rules and regulations, and school policies. In addition, students study diverse learning and teaching styles as well as explore the meaning and the reality of creating an environment that is conducive to and supportive of infused multicultural education.

title: Overview of Assistive Technology
description: This course examines the applications of assistive technology for individuals with disabilities as it relates to teaching and learning. Existing research with students with special needs will be reviewed and new applications of existing and developing technology will be explored.

title: Human Relations     1-3 credit hours
description: This course meets the Nebraska Department of Education Human Relations requirement for certificate renewal. Participants will develop skills in guiding student learning in an environment of respect for diversity, personal values, and heritage. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00

title: Curriculum Implementation
description: This course addresses the curriculum implementation process, theoretical concepts associated with the implementation process, and related implementation issues, including the contested nature of the curriculum and the relationship between curriculum and equity issues. This information is used to critique current curriculum implementation efforts in the public school setting and to develop curriculum for use in the classroom. The course includes a writing component.

title: Principles of Instructional Design
description: This course introduces key principles of instructional design and their application in teaching and learning. Students will explore and evaluate various instructional design models, focusing on the steps involved in designing, developing, revising, implementing, and assessing instruction for diverse learners and contexts. Through this process, educators will identify the model that best supports their professional setting and goals.

title: The Effective Teacher: Enhancing Classroom Instruction
description: This course is designed to provide participants with research and experience based information, strategies, techniques and hands-on activities they can use to improve and enrich the quality of their teaching and to increase the probability of learning and success of their students.

title: Practicum: Education
description: This course is designed to provide a practical experience for students in the area(s) of Early Childhood Inclusive, specific subject endorsement areas, the post-baccalaureate endorsement in teaching English as a second language, or for individuals who have allowed their teaching certificate to expire and are seeking re-certification.  Full time teaching is to be completed by the student for the number of weeks assigned based on the educational program.  Students will be directly supervised by their cooperating teachers (where applicable) and university faculty. The specific setting will be determined by the supervising faculty from the University and the student. Total Credits Allowed: 18.00

title: Practicum: Reading and Literacy
description: The reading practicum involves supervised literacy assessment and tutoring of struggling literacy students from two different levels: the PK-6 level and the 7-12 level. Advanced teacher candidates will administer and score various literacy assessment instruments for PK-12 students. Advanced teacher candidates will then use the resulting assessment data to plan, develop, and teach a series of appropriate tutoring lessons for the PK-12 students, using their instructional strengths to address instructional needs. The final project will be written case study reports for two PK-12 students, one from each level. Prerequisite: TE 845 and TE 846 or department permission

title: Practicum: Reading-Primary K-3
description: This course focuses on the application of the newest innovations in reading and language arts instruction. This involves supervised remedial tutoring of individual children or small groups of children having similar reading disabilities. The course also provides opportunities for the student to evaluate and assess the reading problems of primary school children (K-3) and to select materials for remediation. Prerequisite: TE 845 and TE 846

title: Practicum: Reading-Intermediate 4-6
description: This course focuses on the application of the newest innovations in reading and language arts instruction. This involves supervised remedial tutoring of individual children or small groups of children having similar reading disabilities. The course also provides opportunities for the student to evaluate and assess the reading problems of intermediate school children (4-6) and to select materials for remediation Prerequisite: TE 845 and TE 846

title: Practicum: Reading-Middle/Secondary 7-12
description: This course focuses on the application of the newest innovations in reading and language arts instruction. This involves supervised remedial tutoring of individual children or small groups of children having similar reading disabilities. The course also provides opportunities for the student to evaluate and assess the reading problems of middle school/secondary students and to select materials for remediation Prerequisite: TE 845 and TE 846

title: Curriculum and Instruction Capstone
description: This course emphasizes the application of knowledge and skills from the student's Curriculum and Instruction Graduate Program and chosen concentration area. Students design and carry out an instructional project that involves planning and implementing an intervention, collecting and analyzing P-12 student learning data, and receiving ongoing review and feedback from the instructor throughout the process. A minimum grade of a B must be earned in this course. Department Consent Required Prerequisite: TE 800

title: Teacher Leadership
description: Effective leaders are needed in our communities, businesses, and schools, as well as in our government agencies and our social institutions if our democratic society is to meet the economic and social challenges generated from a constantly changing world. As schools continue to change and evolve to address a variety of learning, human, and community needs teachers have become instrumental in leading and managing changes in their classrooms, in their schools, and in their communities; consequently, the demands associated with teacher leadership will continue to change as well. Accordingly, the preparation of teacher leaders should focus on the development of a broadly applicable base of knowledge and skills for those serving in a variety of roles. This class is designed to assist graduate students in developing the knowledge and skills that will enable them to provide the teacher leadership our classrooms, our schools, and our communities need and deserve.

title: Science of Reading
description: The objective of this course is to help teachers increase their knowledge of the process of language acquisition, linguistic, psychological, and cultural concepts of literacy and pedagogy through an in-depth study of the development of brain structure and function. Students will increase their skills evaluating current research for application to educational practice and implementation with diverse learners. The science of reading, writing, teaching, and learning will be addressed.

title: Language Development for Multilingual Learners
description: This graduate-level course explores language development with a focus on phonology, dialects, multilingualism, and the connection between language and culture. It introduces the major domains of language with attention to form (morphology), content (semantics), and use (pragmatics). Spoken and written language development are examined through the lens of bilingual and multilingual acquisition. Candidates will analyze linguistic patterns, developmental processes, and variations across dialects and cultures to better understand how language functions in educational contexts. Emphasis is placed on developing analytical thinking, research-based understanding of language systems, and effective written communication. Through readings, applied analysis, and reflective inquiry, candidates will connect theory to instructional practice in linguistically diverse classrooms.

title: Culture and Advocacy for Multilingual Learners
description: This graduate-level course prepares students for the English as a Second Language (ESL) supplemental endorsement by exploring the cultural, social, and legal dimensions of teaching multilingual learners (MLs) in today's K-12 schools. Through the lenses of culturally sustaining pedagogy, linguistic equity, and social justice advocacy, students will examine how culture, identity, and language intersect to shape students' educational experiences. The course emphasizes understanding demographic shifts, immigration policies, and the historical and current civil rights laws that impact multilingual learners and their families. Students will analyze case studies, reflect on personal and institutional biases, and develop skills to foster inclusive classroom environments where all students feel valued and supported. By the end of the course, candidates will be able to design equitable learning environments, engage effectively with families and communities, and advocate for the rights and success of multilingual learners at the classroom, school, and policy levels.

title: Content Methods & Strategies for Multilingual Learners
description: This graduate-level course focuses on the theories, methods, and instructional strategies used to support multilingual language learners in content-area classrooms. Teacher candidates explore integrative language development with academic content across disciplines, aligning with WIDA English Language Development Standards. This course places emphasis on sheltered instruction, scaffolding, differentiation, and culturally sustaining pedagogy. Candidates will design lessons promoting linguistic and academic growth, apply research-based strategies, such as the SIOP Model and translanguaging, and analyze classroom practices fostering equity and inclusion. Through readings, field applications, and reflection, candidates will learn to create inclusive, linguistically responsive content instruction for multilingual learners.

title: Assessment and Evaluation of Multilingual Learners
description: This graduate-level course examines the principles, purposes, and practices of assessing English language learners (ELLs) and multilingual learners (MLs) across K-12 settings. Teacher candidates explore the theoretical foundations of language assessment, including validity, reliability, and fairness, and learn to interpret and use data to inform instruction. The course emphasizes formative and summative assessment strategies aligned with WIDA English Language Development Standards and state accountability frameworks. Candidates will analyze standardized and classroom-based assessments, develop performance-based tools, and design linguistically and culturally responsive assessment practices that promote equity. Through hands-on activities and critical reflection, candidates will learn to evaluate student progress, communicate results effectively, and apply data for instructional decision-making.

title: Infusing STEM in the K-8 Classroom
description: The Infusing STEM in the K-8 Classroom course is designed to help graduate students develop their knowledge and ability to teach using STEM practices at the elementary or middle school level. The course will emphasize the rationale and techniques used to develop and implement STEM practices in grades K-8. Assignments will involve review of literature, evaluation of curricula, development of teaching activities and involvement in educational technology and its application to teaching and learning. Prerequisite: TE 853D or TE 853F or TE 853G Recommended (one technology course) TE 886 or TE 866 or TE 878

title: Implementing Universal Design for Learning in School Settings
description: Focus on understanding Universal Design for Learning (UDL) and how to implement it effectively in school settings to address needs associated with learner variability.

title: Professional Skills & Knowledge I
description: TE831 is the first of three 6-hour courses which, followed by student teaching, together comprise an alternative pathway to 7-12 teacher certification (6-12, 5-9, and K-12 in some subjects) in Nebraska in selected disciplines. TE831 builds understanding of the community and professional foundations of public schooling and provides information relating to the curriculum strands of Student Development, Learning Differences, Learning Environments, Content Knowledge, Application of Content, Assessment, Planning for Instruction, Instructional Strategies, Professional Learning and Ethical Practice, Leadership and Collaboration, Human Relations, and Special Education. Prerequisite: Admission to Transitional Certification Program

title: Professional Skills and Knowledge II
description: TE832 is the second of three 6-hour courses which, with student teaching, together comprise an alternative pathway to grades 7-12 teacher certification (6-12 and K-12 in some subjects) in Nebraska in selected content endorsement areas. TE832 builds upon the understanding of the foundations of public schooling addressed in TE831 and provides additional training relating to the curriculum strands of Foundations of Education, Methods, and Materials of Teaching, Assessment of Student Learning, and Human Growth and Development. Additionally, TE832 requires a minimum of 35 hours of subject-specific applications in a supervised, directed field experience in an approved classroom setting for those who are not currently employed as teachers. Prerequisite: TE 831 and Admission to the Transitional Certification Program

title: Context of Education
description: TE833 is the third of three 6-hour courses which, with student teaching, together comprise an alternative pathway to 7-12 teacher certification (6-12, 5-9, and PK-12 in some subjects) in Nebraska in selected content endorsement areas. TE833 builds upon the understanding of the community and professional foundations of public schooling addressed in TE831 and TE832, and provides additional training relating to the curriculum strands of Student Development, Learning Differences, Learning Environments, Content Knowledge, Application of Content, Assessment, Planning for Instruction, Instructional Strategies, Professional Learning and Ethical Practice, Leadership and Collaboration, Human Relations, and Special Education. Additionally, for those who are not currently employed as teachers, TE833 requires a minimum of 35 hours of subject-specific field experience in a supervised, directed field experience in an approved classroom setting appropriate to their endorsement area(s). Prerequisite: TE 832 and Admission to the Transitional Certification Program and Admission to Teacher Education Program

title: Clinical Practice 1: Introduction to Transitional Student Teaching
description: TE834 is the first of two 3-hour student teaching experiences for transitional permit teachers which, together with TE835: Clinical Practice 2: Transitional Student Teaching 2, comprise the student teaching requirement for the completion of the professional education sequence in UNK's Teacher Education Program for the transitional pathway to PK-12/6-12/7-12 teacher certification in Nebraska in selected endorsement areas. TE834 and TE835 students are teachers who work under a transitional permit in approved PreK-12 schools, and as such, function as paid interns. Prerequisite: Admission to Transitional Certification Program

title: Clinical Practice 2: Transitional Student Teaching
description: TE835 is the second of two 3-hour student teaching experiences for transitional permit teachers which, together with TE834:  Clinical Practice 1: Introduction to Student Teaching, comprise the student teaching requirement for the completion of the professional education sequence in UNK's Teacher Education Program for the alternative pathway to teacher certification in Nebraska in selected disciplines. TE834 and TE835 students are transitional permit teachers who work full time in approved PreK-12 schools, and as such, function as paid interns. Prerequisite: TE 833 and TE 834 and Admission to Transitional Certification Program and Admission to Teacher Education Program

title: Clinical Practice: Student Teaching
description: TE 836 is the student teaching experience for transitional and alternative permit teachers and for non-employed completers of the professional education sequence in UNK's Teacher Education Program for teacher certification in Nebraska in selected disciplines. TE 836 students who are transitional or alternative permit teachers work in approved PreK-12 schools, and as, such function as paid interns. Prerequisite: Admission to Transitional Certification Program and Completion of Content Coursework and Admission to Teacher Education Program

title: Curriculum and Assessment
description: This course is designed to prepare post-baccalaureate students pursuing Pk-12/Middle Level/Secondary endorsements for the considerable challenges of effectively planning and designing classroom instruction and assessing student progress toward defined objectives. The course will emphasize: planning and preparation, standards-based instruction, assessment alternatives, creating quality assessment tools, standardized testing, and interpreting assessment results in Pk-12/Middle Level/Secondary settings. This course requires 25 hours of practicum experience with a certified classroom teacher in your endorsement area to meet NDE Rule 20 requirements for an initial certification program. Prerequisite: Admission to Transitional Certification Program. Corequisite: TE 840 .

title: Literacy Development
description: This course is designed to introduce practical instructional strategies supported by research and theory for teaching reading and writing in content areas. Inclusive and integrated literacy instruction including differentiation for diverse middle and secondary students will be addressed. This course requires 25 hours of practicum experience with a certified classroom teacher in your endorsement area to meet NDE Rule 20 requirements for an initial certification program. Prerequisite: Admission to Transitional Certification Program and TE 837 and TE 840 . Corequisite: TE 839 .

title: Pedagogies of Culture, Community, and Inclusivity
description: This course explores the principles and practices of multicultural education and culturally responsive pedagogies, equipping students with the skills to create inclusive and engaging learning environments for all students. Participants will learn to recognize and celebrate the diverse cultural backgrounds of their students and incorporate this understanding into their teaching methods, curriculum design, and classroom management. Emphasis will be placed on strategies for fostering meaningful connections between academic content and students' lived experiences, promoting a sense of belonging, and enhancing student achievement. Through research-based frameworks, practical applications, and engagement with peers, students will deepen their abilities to center the strengths and needs of each learner through responsive approaches to teaching and learning.  This course requires 25 hours of practicum experience with a certified classroom teacher in your endorsement area to meet NDE Rule 20 requirements for an initial certification program. Prerequisite: Admission to Transitional Certification Program and TE 837 and TE 840 . Corequisite: TE 838 .

title: Learning Environments
description: This course is part of the alternative pathway to 7-12 teacher certification (6-12, 5-9, and K-12 in some subjects) in Nebraska in selected disciplines. This course builds understanding of the community and professional foundations of public schooling and provides information relating to the curriculum strands of Student Development, Learning Differences, Learning Environments, Content Knowledge, Application of Content, Assessment, Planning for Instruction, Instructional Strategies, Professional Learning and Ethical Practice, Leadership and Collaboration, Human Relations, and Special Education. This course requires 25 hours of practicum experience with a certified classroom teacher in your endorsement area to meet NDE Rule 20 requirements for an initial certification program. Prerequisite: Admission to Transitional Certification Program. Corequisite: TE 837 .

title: Contemporary Theory & Practice in Literacy
description: Students enrolled in this course will explore several theories as they affect reading and literacy practice. Additionally, students will research a specific reading and literacy theory for additional information and study.

title: Literacy, Assessment, and Instructional Intervention
description: This course focuses on enhancing graduate student knowledge and understanding of a variety of literacy assessments in order to improve literacy abilities of educationally disabled readers and writers. Graduate students enrolled in this course will learn to administer, score, and interpret literacy assessments. They will utilize resulting assessment data to design appropriate instructional interventions. This course is a prerequisite for TE 816B , Reading Practicum.

title: History and Philosophy of the Middle School
description: This course addresses the social, political, and economic forces which accompanied the development of middle schools, along with middle school philosophy and its implications for instructional programs and practices.

title: Formative Assessment & Successful Acad Interventions for Elem School Math
description: This class will emphasize various formative assessment methods for math, diagnosis of common computational errors, and interventions for errors. Types of errors will be illustrated and suggested corrective procedures will be explored utilizing conceptual teaching with appropriate concrete models. Error patterns based on faulty learned mathematical concepts, the corrective procedures for developing or redeveloping those basic mathematical concepts and development of remediation materials will be illustrated. References to the National Council of Teachers of Mathematics will be discussed and emphasized throughout the course.

title: Elementary School Curriculum
description: This class focuses on the principles and trends in elementary curriculum development and a study of the various types of curricula in the elementary school as related to organization patterns. Procedures for continuous evaluation and improvement of local elementary curricula are stressed, including administration and supervision factors and relationship to the affected areas of curriculum.

title: Principles of Early Childhood Education
description: Acquaints students with the knowledge and skills required to develop, administer and supervise early education, childcare, and early intervention programs, serving children with and without disabilities, birth to age 8. Students will implement a strengths-based approach and apply cultural sensitivity to support families from diverse cultural, linguistic, and economic backgrounds and situations.

title: Issues and Trends in Early Childhood Education
description: This course is designed to provide graduate students with an understanding of the political and social issues facing the early childhood field, how these are effecting systemic change, and ways to respond to those changes. Students will also examine a number of historical and current early initiatives and their effect upon young children and families, and evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of various models of early childhood programs.

title: Improvement of Instruction in Early Childhood Education
description: This course is designed to present the most recent research literature and teaching strategies as they relate to the curriculum content advocated for young children, with and without exceptionalities, including those from low-income families or who are linguistically or culturally diverse. Students will learn to evaluate curriculum, and to design, adapt, and implement quality instruction for young children with diverse educational needs.

title: Improvement of Instruction in Mathematics Grades PK-8
description: This interactive and collaborative course will focus on improving classroom instruction for preschool, elementary, and middle school children up to grade 8. Students will access multiple resources available to mathematics teachers in order to broaden and deepen their understanding of developmentally appropriate and differentiated learning experiences for their own students. Students will explore and be encouraged to utilize the latest research-based teaching and assessment methods as advocated by the National Council of the Teachers of Mathematics (NCTM), as well as examine and revise their own personal philosophy of teaching mathematics.

title: Improvement of Instruction in Elementary School Language Arts
description: Attention is directed to the newest innovations in language arts instruction, (reading, writing, listening, speaking) and to the selection of materials for diagnostic and instructional programs. Students will evaluate the various teaching techniques through a comparative study of current programs in elementary language arts, (Reading, writing, listening, speaking).

title: Improvement of Instruction in Elementary School Science
description: An examination of current literature and practices is made relative to science content, methods, and materials in elementary school science.

title: Improvement of Instruction in Elementary School Social Studies
description: Students completing this course will have an opportunity to examine current literature and programs in elementary social studies and to reevaluate their teaching of the social studies.

title: Improvement of Instruction in Mathematics for Grades PK-3
description: This course will focus on improving classroom instruction beginning in the primary grades up to grade 3 as advocated by the National Council of the Teachers of Mathematics (NCTM). Students will be provided with the opportunity to become familiar with many of the resources available to mathematics teachers as they broaden their understanding of developmentally appropriate learning experiences for your students (which utilize the latest research-based teaching and assessment methods). Students will also examine and revise their personal philosophy of teaching mathematics in a classroom in this supportive, interactive and collaborative course.

title: Improvement of Mathematics Instruction for Grades 4-8
description: This course will focus on improving classroom instruction in the Intermediate and Middle School grades (4th-8th) as advocated by the National Council of the Teachers of Mathematics (NCTM). Students will be provided with the opportunity to become familiar with many of the resources available to intermediate and middle school mathematics teachers as they broaden their understanding of developmentally appropriate learning experiences for their students (which utilize the latest research-based teaching and assessment methods). Students will also examine and revise their personal philosophy of teaching mathematics in a Intermediate classroom in this supportive, interactive and collaborative course.

title: Disciplinary Literacy
description: Students completing this course will explore the nature of the reading and writing processes and the literacy needs that middle and secondary students encounter in content areas. This course will also provide teachers with the knowledge and understanding of disciplinary literacy and specific instructional strategies that support student learning in disciplinary content areas.

title: Early Communication, Language and Literacy
description: Students will gain an understanding of early speech and language development and related emergent language and literacy skills, and how these may be influenced by cultural, linguistic, or exceptionality factors. Students will learn how to develop, evaluate, and implement early literacy materials and assessments for children with diverse educational needs ages 0-5 in program, school, and clinical settings.

title: The Resilient Educator
description: This course examines the interdisciplinary study of happiness and well-being, addressing theoretical frameworks, empirical research, and practical applications in the context of being an educator in today's ever-changing and fast-paced classrooms and schools. Drawing on findings from psychology, neuroscience, and the social sciences, learners will explore diverse definitions of happiness and evaluate the biological, sociocultural, and economic influences that shape human flourishing. Emphasis is placed on applying this knowledge to real-world contexts, with attention to balancing short-term goals with long-term meaning and purpose. Learners will also evaluate the role of relationships, bodily health, and material pursuits in overall life satisfaction. The course culminates in the design of a personalized happiness and well-being plan, enabling students to synthesize research, individual values, and the unique benefits and challenges of being an educator. The course includes a reflective writing component.

title: Introduction to Gaming in Education
description: This course will introduce fundamental theories of game design and their application in education. Students will learn how to select and evaluate educational games for specific learning objectives and subject matters; design engaging educational games with appropriate goals, challenges, rewards, and feedback; rapidly prototype game ideas for testing and promotion; and create simple educational games/simulations that can be used in classroom.

title: Digital Storytelling
description: This course focuses on storytelling as a digital medium; as a powerful strategy for individual, library, and classroom instruction; and as a persuasive tool in educational settings. Students will design curriculum-based instruction using digital storytelling strategies for use with students in the school library or classroom setting, as well as learn to create personal and persuasive digital stories. Prerequisite: Graduate status

title: Motivating the 21st Century Learner
description: This course focuses on exploring research-based principles for motivating PK-12 students to foster in them a disposition of lifelong learning. Topics include strategies for fostering intrinsic motivation, motivating the reluctant learner, using technology to stimulate learning and maintaining the educators' motivation. The course emphasizes the practical application of strategies customized to the students' field of interest.

title: Traditional Storytelling
description: This course focuses on storytelling as an oral tradition and as a powerful tool for differentiating instruction. Students will design curriculum-based instruction using storytelling strategies for use with students in the school library or classroom setting, as well as with special needs audiences. Additionally, students will have the opportunity to learn traditional storytelling techniques in a friendly, low-key environment. Prerequisite: Graduate status

title: Copyright, Fair Use, and Ethics
description: This course focuses on the ethical use of copyrighted material, Fair Use Guidelines, and plagiarism as they relate to teaching, training, and instructional design. The impact of artificial intelligence on copyright laws and policies is discussed at length in the course.

title: Introduction to School Library Program
description: This course is an overview of selected school library topics: current trends, genres, issues, instructional and motivational strategies in children's and adolescent literature, along with an introduction to information literacy; school library and educational professional ethics including issues concerning the individual, society, and the profession are introduced.

title: Developing Web-Based Portfolios
description: This course will focus on planning, revising, and developing web-based working and academic portfolios. Tools such as web editors and image editing software will be used. Student will create a working portfolio to use during their academic programs.

title: Collection Development and Management
description: Collection Development and Management is designed to provide the student with the basic competencies needed for print and non-print collection selection, development and evaluation. Prerequisite: TE 869

title: Organization of School Library and Technology Resources
description: Students will learn a variety of techniques for providing access to resources, including exploring the types and functions of automation systems and basic methods and procedures of cataloging and classification within a school library program. Prerequisite: TE 869

title: Reference Services and Resources
description: This course will focus on the range of reference resources that support the school library media center, including the evaluation and use of print and electronic reference sources, reference procedures, artificial intelligence, and the instructional role of reference resources in the overall school curriculum. Prerequisite: TE 869

title: Production of Instructional Resources
description: This course will focus on instructional design principles and will develop competencies in applying them to specific learning objectives within the overall production process. In addition, students will develop skills in integrating the technology products into the curriculum.

title: Administration of the School Library
description: This course will focus on the administration of the school library program's mission, budgeting, advocacy, supervision of personnel, planning, resource needs, managing Artificial Intelligence, communication within the school and public communities, the role of the information specialist in the instructional process, and the interrelationships of information agencies. Prerequisite: TE 869

title: Integration of Curriculum and Digital Technology
description: This course focuses on the practical application of theories of instruction and technology integration, models of teaching, artificial intelligence, curriculum design and research paradigms, as well as studies and findings related to effective curriculum and technology integration for educators.

title: Digital and Online Learning Environments
description: This course is for those who wish to use digital environments and online learning platforms to best meet diverse student needs. Students will create, implement and evaluate student-centered interactive learning environments, strategies and online learning platforms.

title: Leadership in Learning Design and Technology
description: This course will provide students with the skills and tools necessary to be an effective, innovative instructional leader in the field of LDT. Technology planning, implementing, evaluating, and funding in LDT will be emphasized.

title: Seminar in Instructional Technology
description: This is a variable content course which focuses on advanced topics in instructional technology. Students will critically analyze issues and problems in the discipline. Empirically testable research questions related to the topics will be identified. This course may be repeated, but only six hours may count towards a degree.

title: Educational Leadership in Artificial Intelligence
description: This course is an in-depth study of the administration and leadership of technology in P-16 educational settings using the potential of artificial intelligence (AI) for efficiency and decision-making. This course explores the intersection of leadership in education and the transformative potential of artificial intelligence (AI). Designed for future educational leaders, administrators, and policymakers, the course examines how AI technologies can enhance decision-making, equity, and efficiency in public education systems. Students will delve into key topics such as data-driven leadership, ethical considerations in AI adoption, and the role of AI in addressing challenges like resource allocation, personalized learning, and teacher support.

title: Online Teaching Instructional Design
description: This course will focus on preparing to and delivering online instruction, online instructional design, and related teaching strategies.

title: Professional Development and Training in Learning Design and Technology
description: This course is designed to equip aspiring leaders with the skills and knowledge needed to provide professional development training in LDT for P-16 educators. Through hands-on experience with contemporary LDT in a transcurricular setting, students will learn how to train classroom teachers to effectively integrate technology into their respective disciplines. The course will explore a variety of learning and teaching strategies with a focus on modeling and promoting effective technology use for learning. Students will also learn to design appropriate integration learning opportunities for staff, taking into consideration adult learning styles and techniques, as well as formative and summative assessments aligned with content and technology standards.

title: Online Teaching Methodology
description: This course will focus on online engagement, online course development, and online teaching strategies. Research-based structural, ethical, legal, and social issues of teaching online will be examined.

title: Instructional Video Production
description: This course will prepare the student to design, create, produce and edit instructional videos. The focus will be on using currently available classroom equipment for video production, editing, and related concepts.

title: Digital Technology for Educators
description: This course is a general introductory course with two areas of focus. As the name implies, one of the areas is technology tools and the other area is educators. The goal of the course is to help us to develop a vision of how we can use technology tools in our roles as educators.

title: Electronic Media Production
description: This course will focus on producing effective electronic presentations. Topics will include: computer graphics; the psychology of color usage; hypertext linkages; screen design and layout; and integrating video, sound, clip art and animation into presentations.

title: Enhancing Classroom Instruction Using Imaging Tools
description: Students will plan, design, develop and manage major instructional video projects for use in their classrooms or training environments.

title: Creating Instructional Videos to Enhance Classroom Instruction
description: Students will plan, design, develop and manage major instructional video projects for use in their classrooms or training environments.

title: Internship in Instructional Technology     1-6 credit hours
description: This course is intended to allow students to participate in various real-life experiences in IT settings. Projects and internships will be selected and conducted under the guidance of a supervising professor. Focus areas will range from interning in technology centers in school districts and businesses to creating innovative classroom and/or hypermedia projects. Students, with the consent of the professor, can take one, two or three hours. This course may be repeated, but only six hours will be counted towards the degree. Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Field Experiences in School Library
description: This course is the capstone experience for students in the MSED-Learning Design & Technology (School Library Concentration), MAED-Curriculum & Instruction (School Library Concentration), or School Library Endorsement programs. Students will gain experience in school library settings. Prerequisite: TE 869 and TE 871 and TE 872 and TE 873 and TE 875

title: Capstone Project in Learning Design and Technology
description: The course goal is to allow students to gain practical experience in their working context by working with the instructor to develop capstone projects in learning design and technology. Students will explore research-based learning theories and instructional models to design meaningful and effective learning opportunities for different learners across different contexts. The specific experiences to be gained and the setting will be determined by the supervising professor and the students. Department Consent Required Prerequisite: TE 800

title: Seminar in Student Teaching Supervision
description: This class is designed to aid present and prospective supervisory teachers. The student will review current literature, become acquainted with techniques, plans and procedures for supervising student teachers, analyze current practices in supervision, and develop materials for use with student teachers.

title: Thesis     3-6 credit hours
description: Student investigates a research problem related to education, following APA style of reporting the research. The student enrolls with the faculty member directing the thesis. Three to 6 credit hours per semester, with a total of 6 hours. Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Seminar in Reading and Literacy
description: This course is designed to examine some of the historical, theoretical, and sociological reading and literacy research over the years, which will help educational professionals to better understand trends and issues that we see in literacy development and research today and their impact on literacy practice. Additionally, students will select a specific topic of current literacy research interest for more in-depth study.

title: Independent Study     1-3 credit hours
description: The focus of this course is an independent investigation into a topic selected by the student and approved by the instructor. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00

title: Special Topics     1-3 credit hours
description: This course serves identified needs and interests of specific target groups. Content of the course, dates, and time arrangements are determined by the Department of Teacher Education. Total Credits Allowed: 24.00

title: Practicum in Special Education     3-6 credit hours
description: This course is designed to provide practicum experience in the endorsement and graduate programs of Special Education Generalist or Advanced Practitioner. Candidates are expected to have an on-site supervisor in addition to supervision by university faculty. Candidates are expected to document direct contact with students with disabilities in their endorsement area. This course is intended to be taken toward the end of your program of study. Department Consent Required Total Credits Allowed: 24.00

title: Consult & Collaboration with Families & Agencies Serv Individuals with Disabilities
description: This course provides an examination of programs and support services for people with disabilities with emphasis on relationship building, promoting inclusivity and its impact on the school environment. The course highlights the significance of learning and responsive teaching methods while also touching upon confidentiality concerns, the need for self-expression, and ethical decision-making skills in educational settings. It stresses the value of working and resolving conflicts in achieving outcomes and creating supportive professional learning environments. Candidates will implement strategies grounded in evidence-based research to demonstrate professional development and conduct for diversity and inclusivity in their work.

title: Medical Aspects of Individuals with Disabilities
description: This course provides teacher candidates with a general knowledge of the medical conditions seen in students with disabilities. It will familiarize candidates with terminology, medications, procedures, equipment, and evidence-based interventions used for students with medical complications. Professional responsibilities regarding medical conditions will be covered.

title: Bilingual Special Education
description: Candidates will learn about the latest research on the education of English language learners with disabilities, including their identification, assessment, and instruction. Special emphasis will be placed on creating effective Individualized Education Plans (IEPs) that incorporate culturally responsive teaching practices in the continuum of educational placements.

title: Legal Issues in Special Education and IEP Development
description: This course provides students with the knowledge and understanding of the history and federal regulations in the field of special education. Professional ethics and code of conduct will be emphasized. Students will explore the foundations for various mandated activities such as parents' rights, IEP components and monitoring, special education placement procedures, and mandated timelines. This course will emphasize the processes and procedures required to meet the State and Federal guidelines for providing appropriate Special Education Services to children with disabilities. The main focus will center on understanding the connection between the legal background and history and mandated requirements within the field.

title: Formal and Informal Assessment in Special Education
description: This course introduces the assessment process in special education, emphasizing procedural safeguards, data collection through informal and standardized methods, and critical issues in assessment. Candidates will explore the psychometric properties of standardized tests and gain hands-on experience in the administration, scoring, and interpretation of selected instruments. The course also includes a comprehensive focus on Response to Intervention (RtI) and Multi-Tiered Systems of Support (MTSS).

title: Research-Based Instructional Strategies for Students with Disabilities
description: This course is designed to provide teacher candidates the knowledge of a wide range of evidence-based academic instructional strategies for students with disabilities. Teacher candidates will learn to synthesize and evaluate research on instructional techniques for classroom application. Candidates will apply their knowledge of lesson development and classroom management strategies in their field placement. Contains a field component.

title: Field Experience in Special Education
description: This course is designed to provide practical application of learned skills from previous classes. Teacher candidates will complete this in a K-8 special education setting for a total of 50 clock hours. Candidates will develop a unit plan and will design and implement lesson plans.

title: Functional Behavior Assessment and Behavior Intervention Planning
description: This graduate-level course is designed for candidates to explore and practice the function-based behavior process for creating a Functional Behavior Analysis and Behavior Intervention Plan. Candidates will gain knowledge of ethical standards for behavior management, crisis intervention, and will research evidence-based intervention strategies for the classroom. Positive behavioral support will be highlighted. This course contains a functional assessment/intervention field component. Department Consent Required

title: Strategies for Teaching Students with Intellectual Disabilities
description: This course prepares candidates to support individuals with intellectual disabilities through targeted evidence-based interventions in the life skills areas, including in the community. Strategies for developing study skills, evaluating alternate curricula, assessment planning, designing accommodations and modifications to foster inclusion in general education will be emphasized. Key focus areas include understanding federal and state transition laws, effective transition planning, interagency collaboration, and utilizing resources and services to support successful transitions to post-school education and employment.

title: Preparing Adolescents for the Post-Secondary World
description: This course is designed to prepare teacher candidates to work with various populations of adolescents with exceptionalities in inclusive settings. This course will prepare candidates for their role in the transition planning process, the students' post-secondary outcomes (post-secondary education, employment or training, independent living), and the impact on the family systems.

title: Teaching Social Skills to Students with Autism Spectrum Disorder
description: This course offers a thorough exploration of autism spectrum disorders (ASD), equipping candidates with essential knowledge and tools to support individuals with autism in various settings. Candidates will learn about the characteristics, identification, and diagnosis of ASD, as well as evidence-based instructional strategies, social skills training, and communication interventions tailored to individual needs. The curriculum covers effective teaching techniques, the impact of autism on families, and transition strategies throughout the lifespan. Topics include assistive technology, motor skills development, academic interventions, and Universal Design for Learning (UDL). Candidates will also investigate research on social skills deficits and educational needs, focusing on tools that foster success.

title: Critical Issues in Special Education
description: This course provides students with the knowledge and understanding of the critical issues facing the field of Special education. Professional ethics and code of conduct will be emphasized.

title: Characteristics and Strategies for individuals with Emotional and Behavioral Health Disorders
description: This course provides candidates with an in-depth coverage of the identification process, etiologies and characteristics associated with disorders that are categorized as learning disabilities or behavior disorders. This course will examine evidence-based research for working with students that have behavioral and emotional disabilities. Interventions appropriate for the three tiers of intervention will be explored.

title: Applied Behavior Analysis
description: This course provides an in-depth examination of Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA). Candidates will examine the history and development of ABA as well as the use of ABA in the education of and research with individuals with disabilities.

title: Intervention Strategies for Individuals with Behavioral & Emotional Disabilities
description: This course will examine effective strategies and interventions for working with students that have behavioral and emotional disabilities. Interventions appropriate for the three tiers of intervention will be explored.

title: Inclusion and Differentiation Strategies for the 21st Century Classroom
description: The course trains teacher candidates with the knowledge and skills of the instructional needs of students with exceptionalities (disabilities and giftedness) as well as those from culturally and linguistically diverse backgrounds in integrated settings. Special education referral process, MTSS, Section 504, analysis of instruction/environment and its impact on learning, assignment/assessment modification, behavioral issues, and evaluation of student learning will be the emphasis. Evidence-based intervention strategies to differentiate instruction will also be discussed.

title: Positive Behavior Intervention and Supports
description: This course presents the latest in practices and research in the field of Positive Behavior Intervention and Supports (PBIS). Students will be exposed to the PBIS model and how it fits into MTSS, creating proactive learning environments through universal support systems and interventions, assessing and monitoring behavior in the school and classroom, and targeted supports for all students with behavioral needs.

title: Special Topics     1-3 credit hours
description: Study in a specific area of theatre which will vary each time the course is offered. Topics of study may include performance, drama therapy, history, play development, technical theatre, theatrical design, as well as others.

title: Expressive Arts and the Creative Teacher
description: This completely online course has been designed to further enrich the graduate level teacher's growth by introducing "the creative connection" (Natalie Rogers). The modules take the student through a firsthand experiential exploration of incorporating the expressive arts, such as movement/dance, art, theatre and music as they go down the rabbit hole into understanding oneself as an educator and how to use the arts in the classroom. An emphasis will be placed on solving creative problems through 3 MAIN UNITS of learning through 6 modules.

title: Seminar in Vocational Education
description: Concerned with the analysis of current problems, trends and recent developments in vocational and technical education with particular attention to situations related to individual student needs and interests. Prerequisite: Graduate standing or permission of instructor

title: Practicum in Vocational Education     3-6 credit hours
description: Designed to provide vocational educators an opportunity to implement new programs, become involved in related research or gain internship experiences. A proposal must be submitted prior to registering for the course. Total Credits Allowed: 6.00 Prerequisite: Graduate standing or permission of instructor

title: Individual Studies in Vocational Education     1-3 credit hours
description: Students spend time on a problem which is not included under the regular vocational/technical education program of instruction. Two copies of a well-written paper must be filed before credit is granted, one to be filed with the professor in charge of the study and one with the Director of the Center for Vocational Education. Offered by arrangement. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00

title: Foundations and Contemporary Issues in Vocational Education     1-3 credit hours
description: Origins and philosophy of vocational education and its relationship to the school curriculum. Required for vocational endorsement and recommended as an elective for school administrators. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00

title: Introduction to Student with Special Needs
description: *(meets the Human Relations requirement for teachers)  This course is designed to assist teachers in understanding some of the causative factors for students' failure in the regular classroom. Emphasis is placed upon issues and concerns in providing education in a multicultural society. Attention is directed towards state and federal legislation regarding disadvantaged and handicapped students and discrimination as it relates to the classroom. The effect of teacher attitude and behavior upon student learning is surveyed.

title: Equity in Today's Society
description: To increase awareness of the Equal Opportunity Legislation, the equity emphasis in the Vocational Education Legislation, and to survey the underlying attitudes and values of society that make it so difficult to achieve the full implementation of equal opportunity.

title: Thesis     3-6 credit hours
description: Total Credits Allowed: 6.00

title: Special Topics in Vocational Education     1-3 credit hours
description: This course is designed to enable students to become knowledgeable of recent trends and issues in vocational education. Total Credits Allowed: 3.00
